name: Beaver Dam Wash
short_description: BLM-managed wash in southwestern Utah offering excellent opportunities to find colorful agates, jasper, and chalcedony in desert washes. Free rockhounding with no permit required.
description:
Beaver Dam Wash is a productive rockhounding destination on Utah BLM land in Washington County, located in the Virgin River basin of southwestern Utah. The wash cuts through geology rich in silica-based minerals deposited during ancient volcanic and hydrothermal activity. Collectors regularly find nodular agates, colorful jasper specimens, and chalcedony fragments in the wash gravels and exposed banks. The site is accessible year-round and ideal for casual rockhounders seeking quality specimens without the cost of commercial fee digs.

The geology of this area reflects the Basin and Range province, where Miocene-era volcanic activity and subsequent erosion have exposed mineral-bearing formations. Silica-rich groundwater moving through fractures and cavities in volcanic host rock precipitated the agates and chalcedony nodules found here today. The wash environment naturally concentrates loose stones, making surface collecting productive.

Visitors should focus their search in the wash bottom and along eroded banks where material concentrates. Early morning is best for avoiding heat and securing the best stones before other collectors work the area. The terrain is relatively open with scattered vegetation, and footing is uneven with rounded cobbles and gravel.
history: This region lies within the Virgin River drainage system, where Miocene volcanic rocks and silica-rich fluids created ideal conditions for agate and chalcedony precipitation. Centuries of erosion through Beaver Dam Wash have liberated these nodules from host rock and concentrated them in the streambed, making surface collecting highly productive.
tips: Search the wash bottom and inside bend banks where water flow concentrates heavier stones. Look for nodular agate with colorful banding, often found partially buried in gravel - use a pry bar to check likely spots. Bring a classifier or mesh screen to help spot translucent specimens in loose material. Early morning collecting yields better results as stones are easier to spot in low-angle light.
terrain_notes: The wash sits at approximately 2400 feet elevation in open desert with sparse creosote and blackbrush vegetation. The streambed is relatively flat with a sandy-gravelly bottom and exposed reddish-brown banks showing layers of volcanic rock. Walking surfaces are uneven with rounded cobbles and loose gravel requiring sturdy boots. Parking to the wash is typically within 0.5 miles of established pullouts.
best_season: October - April. Daytime temperatures in summer exceed 100 degrees Fahrenheit, making winter and early spring ideal for extended rockhounding sessions. Flash flood risk in the wash is lower during dry months.
written_directions: From Washington, Utah, take Highway 91 north approximately 20 miles toward the Arizona border. Look for dirt road access points on the east side of the highway near mile marker 17-18. Turn east and follow rough roads toward Beaver Dam Wash. Parking areas are informal pullouts; multiple access points exist along the wash system. Standard passenger vehicles can reach some entry points, but high-clearance vehicles are recommended for deeper wash access.
road_conditions: Dirt and gravel roads, rough washboard surface in places. High-clearance vehicle recommended for some access points. Standard passenger cars can reach main collecting areas via highway pullouts.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along Highway 91 and side roads. No developed parking lot. Multiple access points available; space typically available.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Pack out all trash. Respect private property boundaries adjacent to BLM land.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Pack out all trash
Respect adjacent private property
nearest_services: Washington, Utah has gas stations, restaurants, and services approximately 20 miles south. Hurricane, Utah offers full services including grocery stores and hotels.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with uneven rocky footing. No wheelchair access to wash. Sturdy hiking boots required. High heat exposure in summer months. Most collecting areas within 0.5 miles of parking pullouts.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can you find at Beaver Dam Wash?
faq.answer: Beaver Dam Wash is known for colorful nodular agates with red, orange, and tan banding, along with various jasper specimens including red and brown varieties. Chalcedony and common quartz are also abundant in the wash gravels. Most agates are found as weathered nodules ranging from fist-sized to grapefruit-sized pieces.

faq.question: Is there a rockhounding permit required for Beaver Dam Wash in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Beaver Dam Wash. The site is on Utah BLM land where personal use collection is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). You can collect up to 25 pounds per day at no cost, with no commercial use allowed.

faq.question: How do you find agate nodules in Beaver Dam Wash?
faq.answer: Focus your search in the wash bottom and along the inside bend of curves where water flow concentrates heavier stones. Agates are nodular and often partially buried in gravel. Use a pry bar to check likely spots around larger cobbles. Early morning light makes spotting translucent specimens easier. Screen or classify loose gravelly material to identify stones you might otherwise overlook.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Beaver Dam Wash?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for collecting at Beaver Dam Wash. Summer temperatures exceed 100 degrees Fahrenheit, making winter and spring much more comfortable for extended rockhounding sessions. Fall and early spring also offer lower flash flood risk in the wash.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should you bring to Beaver Dam Wash?
faq.answer: Bring a pry bar or small crowbar to check loose cobbles, a mesh classifier to sift gravelly material, sturdy hiking boots, plenty of water, sunscreen, and a hat. A 5-gallon bucket is useful for collecting and carrying specimens. A loupe helps evaluate agates for quality and translucency. A GPS or map is helpful since the wash has multiple access points.

faq.question: Is Beaver Dam Wash a good rockhounding site for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Beaver Dam Wash is excellent for beginners. The BLM site requires no permit, has a clear 25-pound daily limit, and surface collecting is very productive. The terrain is relatively open and easy to walk, and the colorful agates and jasper are straightforward to identify. Families with children ages 6 and up find this a rewarding and accessible destination.

meta_title: Beaver Dam Wash Rockhounding - Utah Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Beaver Dam Wash, Utah. Find agates, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit required. 25 lb daily limit. Beginner friendly.
